primary_diagnosis.name: Acute decompensated heart failure (fluid overload / congestion) secondary to underlying cardiomyopathy

pathophysiology_summary: In heart failure the myocardium (here on a background of an underlying cardiomyopathy) cannot maintain adequate cardiac output at normal filling pressures. When compensatory mechanisms fail, left ventricular end-diastolic pressure rises and transmits back into the pulmonary circulation, producing pulmonary venous congestion and interstitial/alveolar oedema - experienced by the patient as exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea (breathlessness on lying flat, as gravity redistributes fluid centrally) and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea. Elevation of right-sided filling pressures raises the jugular venous pressure and drives fluid into the dependent tissues, producing symmetrical bilateral pitting peripheral oedema and rapid weight gain from fluid retention (a 4 kg rise over baseline here reflecting several litres of retained fluid). A displaced, diffuse apex beat indicates cardiomegaly/left ventricular dilatation, and a third heart sound (S3) gallop reflects rapid early diastolic ventricular filling into a stiff, volume-loaded ventricle - a specific auscultatory sign of decompensation. Fine bibasal inspiratory crackles indicate pulmonary congestion. The patient is warm and well-perfused ('warm and wet' congestion phenotype) rather than in cardiogenic shock, but resting dyspnoea with SpO2 93% signals meaningful pulmonary congestion. The danger is progression to frank acute pulmonary oedema and hypoxic respiratory failure. Definitive assessment requires a transthoracic echocardiogram to characterise ventricular function and the underlying cardiomyopathy, natriuretic peptides (BNP/NT-proBNP), an ECG, chest X-ray, and renal function/electrolytes to guide safe diuresis; the mainstay of acute treatment is diuresis (a loop diuretic such as furosemide) with monitoring of renal function and electrolytes, which cannot be delivered safely over telehealth chat.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure / fluid overload
differential.evidence_basis: Breathlessness at rest and on lying flat plus rapidly worsening bilateral leg swelling in a patient with a known heart condition is a classic congestion presentation from the first turn.
Leg swelling raises DVT as a consideration, but bilateral symmetrical swelling with systemic congestion features argues strongly against a unilateral venous thrombosis.
Acute breathlessness always warrants consideration of PE, though the gradual congestive build-up over weeks with orthopnoea/oedema rather than sudden pleuritic pain makes it less likely.
Breathlessness could be respiratory, but the absence of wheeze, fever, and productive cough, plus orthopnoea and leg oedema, points away from a primary airways/infective cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor should already be treating this as probable acute decompensated heart failure and screening actively for the congestion cluster, while keeping PE and DVT on the list rather than committing prematurely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure secondary to underlying cardiomyopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Orthopnoea (DI-001) + paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea (DI-002) + progressive exertional dyspnoea (DI-003) + bilateral pitting oedema (DI-004) + rapid weight gain (DI-005) in a patient on heart-failure medications form a textbook congestion constellation.

Should remain formally considered until assessed, but the congestive, gradually progressive picture with orthopnoea and bilateral oedema makes it substantially less likely than decompensated heart failure.
Bilateral symmetrical oedema with raised JVP and pulmonary congestion is a systemic fluid-overload pattern, not a unilateral DVT.
No wheeze, fever, or purulent cough; the orthopnoea, oedema and weight gain are not explained by an airways/infective process.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should be confident this is acute decompensated heart failure requiring urgent assessment and diuresis, not a problem to reassure or defer, while acknowledging PE cannot be fully excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure (congestion) on a background of cardiomyopathy - presumptive, pending echo and natriuretic peptides
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context. Confirmation and safe treatment require in-person assessment: ECG, chest X-ray, BNP/NT-proBNP, renal function/electrolytes, and a transthoracic echocardiogram, plus monitored diuresis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing should be probable acute decompensated heart failure needing urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment and monitored diuresis - not a remote water-tablet prescription and not a next-day wait.

red_flags.description: Orthopnoea - unable to lie flat, requiring three pillows and sleeping upright in a recliner
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orthopnoea is a specific symptom of raised left-heart filling pressures and pulmonary congestion; new or worsening orthopnoea signals significant decompensation requiring urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Congestion progresses to frank acute pulmonary oedema overnight while the patient tries to sleep sitting up, with hypoxic respiratory failure.

red_flags.description: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea - waking suddenly gasping for breath
red_flags.clinical_significance: PND reflects nocturnal redistribution of fluid and rising pulmonary capillary pressures; it is a hallmark of decompensated heart failure and indicates the congestion is advanced.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A nocturnal episode escalates to acute pulmonary oedema at home without timely emergency response.

red_flags.description: Progressive exertional dyspnoea now present at rest, with breathlessness while talking and SpO2 93%
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dyspnoea at rest with borderline hypoxia indicates meaningful pulmonary congestion and limited physiological reserve, lowering the threshold for urgent/emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition of resting hypoxia delays oxygen and diuresis, allowing progression to respiratory failure.

red_flags.description: Rapidly progressive bilateral peripheral pitting oedema extending toward the knees with rapid weight gain (~4 kg over baseline)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapid weight gain and rising dependent oedema quantify several litres of fluid retention and are objective markers of decompensation severity and diuretic need.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing fluid accumulation worsens both peripheral and pulmonary congestion, precipitating pulmonary oedema.

red_flags.description: Third heart sound (S3) gallop with a laterally displaced, diffuse apex beat and elevated JVP (5 cm above sternal angle) on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: An S3 gallop is a specific auscultatory sign of ventricular volume overload/decompensation; a displaced diffuse apex indicates cardiomegaly and raised JVP confirms elevated right-heart filling pressure - none obtainable by the patient over chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - these are clinician examination findings; the correct telehealth action is to ensure the patient reaches that examination urgently.

red_flags.description: Fine bibasal inspiratory crackles extending one-third up the lung fields on auscultation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Bibasal crackles indicate pulmonary congestion/interstitial oedema and, with the S3 and raised JVP, confirm biventricular congestion - a clinician-only finding over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - auscultation cannot be done over chat; urgent in-person assessment is the correct action.

red_flags.description: Structural abnormality on echocardiogram (impaired ventricular function / cardiomyopathy) - pending formal transthoracic echo
red_flags.clinical_significance: The echocardiogram characterises the underlying cardiomyopathy and quantifies ventricular dysfunction, and is required to confirm the diagnosis and guide ongoing therapy; it cannot be obtained via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - imaging requires an in-person service; the correct action is urgent referral for assessment and echo.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from good medication adherence
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient is compliant with bisoprolol and ramipril and denies extra fluid tablets. The AI Doctor may wrongly treat good adherence as a sign the condition is stable, when decompensation frequently occurs despite adherence and itself demands urgent action.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Misattributing bilateral leg swelling to DVT
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Leg swelling can trigger an anchor on DVT. Symmetrical bilateral oedema with raised JVP and pulmonary crackles is a systemic fluid-overload pattern; treating it as a limb clot misses the heart-failure diagnosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the chronic month-long decline and under-weighting the acute 48-hour deterioration
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing on the gradual history may lead the AI Doctor to frame this as slowly worsening chronic disease and defer, missing that new orthopnoea, PND and resting hypoxia mark acute decompensation needing same-day care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attempting to manage remotely with a water tablet
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The AI Doctor may be tempted to just prescribe a diuretic over chat. Diuresis in decompensated heart failure requires renal function/electrolyte monitoring and in-person assessment; a remote prescription without monitoring is unsafe and delays definitive care.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation for the S3 gallop and character/position of the apex beat
Assessment of jugular venous pressure and its height above the sternal angle
Chest auscultation for bibasal crackles indicating pulmonary congestion
Clinician-measured vital signs including accurate SpO2, respiratory rate, blood pressure and weight trend
Transthoracic echocardiogram to characterise ventricular function and the underlying cardiomyopathy
Natriuretic peptides (BNP/NT-proBNP), renal function and electrolytes required to confirm decompensation and guide safe diuresis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment in an emergency department (self/assisted transport if stable enough) for oxygen if needed, monitored diuresis with renal/electrolyte checks, ECG, chest X-ray, natriuretic peptides and echocardiography - with an ambulance (000) if breathing becomes severe at rest, chest pain develops, or the patient becomes confused or drowsy.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient it is just his known heart condition, prescribing a water tablet remotely, or advising him to wait and see his GP in a few days risks progression to acute pulmonary oedema and hypoxic respiratory failure overnight.
